body_text:
Section 5.4 Products of Rational and Trigonometric Functions
3

EXAMPLE 3 Cauchy principal values and singular points
Write down P.V. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{x-1} d x$ in terms of limits of integrals on finite intervals.
Solution The integral is improper because it extends over the infinite real line ar because the integrand is singular at $x=1$. Accordingly, the principal value involv two limits; it is
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { P.V. } \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{x-1} d x= & \lim _{R \rightarrow \infty}\left(\int_{-R}^{0} \frac{1}{x-1} d x+\int_{2}^{R} \frac{1}{x-1} d x\right) \\
& +\lim _{r \rightarrow 0^{+}}\left(\int_{0}^{1-r} \frac{1}{x-1} d x+\int_{1+r}^{2} \frac{1}{x-1} d x\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
al value
nple 3.
where the choices $x=0$ and $x=2$ were arbitrary; any pair of numbers where th first is in $(-\infty, 1)$ and the second is in $(1, \infty)$ will work to split the integrals. Usin elementary methods, we can see that both limits on the right of (14) exist, so th principal value exists, and we can write (Figure 8)
$$
\text { P.V. } \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{x-1} d x=\lim _{R \rightarrow \infty, r \rightarrow 0^{+}}\left(\int_{-R}^{1-r} \frac{1}{x-1} d x+\int_{1+r}^{R} \frac{1}{x-1} d x\right)
$$
where the limits may be taken in any manner. Using basic calculus, we find the for large $R>1$
$$
\int_{-R}^{1-r} \frac{1}{x-1} d x=\left.\ln |x-1|\right|_{-R} ^{1-r}=\ln (r)-\ln (R+1)
$$
and
$$
\int_{1+r}^{R} \frac{1}{x-1} d x=\left.\ln |x-1|\right|_{1+r} ^{R}=\ln (R-1)-\ln (r)
$$

So
$$
\text { P.V. } \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{x-1} d x=\lim _{R \rightarrow \infty}[\ln (R-1)-\ln (R+1)]=\lim _{R \rightarrow \infty} \ln \left(\frac{R-1}{R+1}\right)=0
$$

Now, we return to the integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin x}{x} d x$. Since the integrand is $w$ behaved at $x=0$ and the integral over the infinite interval converges, we c compute it by taking the imaginary part of P.V. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{e^{i x}}{x} d x$. This princi value integral involves the limit as $r \rightarrow 0^{+}$and $R \rightarrow \infty$ of an integral ov $[-R,-r]$ and $[r, R]$. We envision the integral as a contour integral of $t$ complex function $\frac{e^{i z}}{z}$ and close the contour with a large positively orient semicircle of radius $R$ and a small negatively oriented semicircle of radi $r$, which will bypass the problem at 0 . The resulting path $\gamma_{r, R}$ is sho in Figure 9. The limit of the path integral over the small circle will computed using the following lemma.
